nativeRange.habitat: Coastal reefs and estuaries, commonly observed on jetty pylons and rocky bays; depth range approximately 1–10 m. Recorded from Darnley Island (Torres Strait, Queensland) south to Bermagui, southern New South Wales.

diet.notes: Grazes naturally on algae and small invertebrates encrusted on rockwork and submerged structures such as jetty pylons. Generally accepts a varied diet of marine algae, Spirulina-based pellets, frozen mysis and brine shrimp, and other meaty preparations. Supplementing with algae-based foods is commonly advised, particularly in systems with limited natural algal growth.

avoidWith: conspecifics (except established mated pairs in larger systems)
boisterous fast-feeding tankmates that outcompete it at meals

summary: A small subtropical Australian blenny endemic to eastern Australia, New Caledonia and adjacent waters. Generally regarded as peaceful and reasonably hardy, but tends to prefer cooler-than-tropical reef tank temperatures and benefits from a long footprint with abundant rockwork. A tightly sealed lid is generally advised, as blennies have been known to jump.
careNotes:
The Horned Blenny (Parablennius intermedius), also called the False Tasmanian Blenny, is a subtropical species endemic to eastern Australia and adjacent western Pacific waters (New Caledonia, Solomon Islands, Papua New Guinea). It is commonly seen on jetty pylons and rocky/estuarine bays to about 10 m, often in small loose groups.

It tends to perch on rocks and dart between crevices, grazing on algae and small invertebrates encrusted on hard surfaces. Provide ample live rock with crevices and a sand or fine-gravel substrate. Open swimming space along the bottom is appreciated, so an aquarium with a long footprint (around 120 cm or larger) is generally advised, even though the fish itself only reaches ~12 cm.

Temperature preferences differ markedly across sources: Reeflex cites a wild range of about 15.4–25.2 °C consistent with this species' subtropical eastern-Australian distribution, while general aquarium guides recommend 22–28 °C as for typical tropical reef tanks. A practical compromise of roughly 18–25 °C is commonly suggested, biased cooler than a typical tropical reef. Keepers running standard tropical reef temperatures (25–27 °C) at the upper end should be aware this species is naturally adapted to cooler subtropical waters.

Generally considered reef-safe and unaggressive toward corals, polyps, and shrimp, though as with many blennies isolated cases of opportunistic polyp nipping cannot be ruled out. It can be kept singly or, in larger systems, as a mated pair; keeping multiple unpaired individuals in modest tanks tends to lead to territorial conflict.

As with most blennies, this species has been known to jump from open-topped aquariums when startled — a tightly sealed lid or full mesh cover is generally advised.

LiveAquaria's product listing labeled "Horned Blenny" refers to a different species (Hypsoblennius exstochilus, a Caribbean blenny reaching only ~5.7 cm); data on that page was not used for this entry. Husbandry parameters here are drawn from species-specific sources covering Parablennius intermedius.

RESOLVED 2026-06-03: The flagged concern was the conflicting temperature guidance across sources (Reeflex wild range 15.4–25.2 °C vs AquaInfo aquarium recommendation 22–28 °C vs the previous compromise of 18–25 °C). FishBase (fishbase.se) was consulted as the primary taxonomic reference: it reports a preferred temperature range of 21.8–27.9 °C (mean 26.2 °C) for Parablennius intermedius based on its ecological modelling. Wikipedia confirmed size (12 cm TL) and Least Concern status but provided no temperature data. The FishBase preferred range aligns closely with AquaInfo's aquarium recommendation and indicates the previous floor of 18 °C was too low for the species' actual thermal preference. The tank temperature range has been corrected to 22–27 °C: the lower bound reflects FishBase's preferred minimum (~21.8 °C, rounded to 22 °C); the upper bound is set conservatively at 27 °C rather than the FishBase maximum of 27.9 °C, acknowledging this species' subtropical biology and the practical reality that many keepers will run systems at or above 26 °C. The Reeflex wild floor of 15.4 °C represents the species' ecological tolerance limit, not its aquarium-optimal range, and is not appropriate as a tank minimum.
breedingNotes: Oviparous, like other combtooth blennies; eggs are demersal and adhesive, attached to substrate via a filamentous pad or pedestal, and males are reported to guard the nest. Captive breeding of this species has not been widely documented in the aquarium hobby and detailed protocols are not available in the sources consulted.

primary.name: AquaInfo
primary.notes: Used as primary because LiveAquaria's "Horned Blenny" listing actually describes a different species (Hypsoblennius exstochilus, Caribbean). AquaInfo provides species-specific husbandry parameters for Parablennius intermedius (tank size 500 L, temperature 22–28 °C, pH 8.1–8.4, salinity 1.020–1.025).

additional.name: Fishes of Australia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Blenniidae, originally Blennius intermedius Ogilby 1915), max size 12 cm TL, depth range to 10 m, distribution (Darnley Island QLD to Bermagui southern NSW; also New Caledonia and Solomon Islands), IUCN Least Concern, reef-associated habitat on jetty pylons.

additional.name: Australian Museum
additional.notes: Cross-checked max size (12 cm), morphology (broad blunt snout, multilobed tentacle above each eye), distribution endemic to Australia northern QLD to southern NSW, and jetty-pylon habitat.

additional.name: Reeflex
additional.notes: Cross-checked tank size (≥500 L), care difficulty (average), depth (1–10 m), and wild temperature range (15.4–25.2 °C — significantly cooler than the 22–28 °C tropical reef range cited by AquaInfo). Also confirmed general blenny escape/jumping risk warning.

additional.name: nyaquatic.com
additional.notes: Originally assigned secondary; confirmed scientific name, ~12 cm size, omnivore diet (algae + small meaty foods), peaceful temperament, generally reef-safe, easy-to-moderate care. The 30-gallon minimum tank size given here is inconsistent with the 500 L minimum from AquaInfo and Reeflex; AquaInfo/Reeflex were favored as the species-specific consensus.

fish.breedingNotes: Oviparous, demersal adhesive eggs attached to substrate via a filamentous pad; not widely captive-bred in the hobby.
